coredata.dc:title: An in-situ strategy to construct uracil-conjugated covalent organic frameworks with tunable fluorescence/recognition characteristics for sensitive and selective Mercury(II) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Xi-Rui
Hu, A-Wei
Hu, Sheng-Qian
Yang, Wen-Li
Sun, Chen
Xiao, Sai-Jin
Yang, Gui-Ping
Zheng, Qiong-Qing
Liang, Ru-Ping
Zhang, Li
Qiu, Jian-Ding
coredata.dc:description: Previous researches of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have shown their potential as fluorescent probes, but the regulation of their optical properties and recognition characteristics still remains a challenge, and most of reports required complicated post-decoration to improve the sensing performance. In this context, we propose a novel in-situ strategy to construct uracil-conjugated COFs and modulate their fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective mercury(II) detection. By using 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-formylphenyl)pyrene (TFPPy) and 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-aminophenyl)pyrene (TAPPy) as fundamental blocks and 5-aminouraci (5-AU) as the functional monomer, a series of COFs (Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5) with tunable fluorescence were solvothermally synthesized through an in-situ Schiff base reaction. The π-conjugated framework serves as a signal reporter, the evenly and densely distributed uracil acts as a mercury(II) receptor, and the regular pores (channels) make the rapid and sensitive detection of the mercury(II) possible. In this research, we manage to regulate the crystalline structure, the fluorescence properties, and the sensing performance of COFs by simply changing the molar ratio of precursors. We expect this research to open up a new strategy for effective and controllable construction of functionalized COFs for environmental analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent organic frameworks
Uracil
In-situ modification
Fluorescence
Mercury(II) detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21964011 , 22166024 , 22064001 and 22036003 ) and Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( 20212ACB203009 and 20212ACB203011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The building units of the Schiff base reaction. (b) Synthetic routes and fragments for Py-COFs. (c) Synthetic routes and fragments for Py-U-COFs.

(a) FT-IR spectra for TFPPy, TAPPy, 5-AU, Py-COFs, and Py-U-COFs. (b) 13C CP/MAS NMR spectra of Py-U-COFs. (c) Experimentally observed PXRD patterns of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-3 (orange and blue, respectively), Pawley refined of Py-COFs (pink), predicted PXRD pattern of Py-COFs (green), Bragg position of Py-COFs (red) and difference of Py-COFs (purple). (d) Nitrogen adsorption isotherms for Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-3. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The SEM images of the Py-COFs (a), Py-U-COFs-1 (b), Py-U-COFs-2 (c), Py-U-COFs-3 (d), Py-U-COFs-4 (e), and Py-U-COFs-5 (f). The scale bar is 1 μm.

Fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) spectra of Py-COFs (a), Py-U-COFs-1 (b), Py-U-COFs-2 (c), Py-U-COFs-3 (d), Py-U-COFs-4 (e), and Py-U-COFs-5 (f). The photographs of COFs in aqueous solutions under the sunlight (g) and the 365 nm UV lamp (h), a to f correspond to Py-COFs to Py-U-COFs-5, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The fluorescence emission spectra of Py-COFs (a), Py-U-COFs-1 (b), Py-U-COFs-2 (c), Py-U-COFs-3 (d), Py-U-COFs-4 (e), and Py-U-COFs-5 (f) upon gradual addition of mercury(II). Inset shows the calibration plot for mercury(II) detection. The excitation wavelength was 360 nm and the fluorescent probe concentration was 0.02 mg/mL.

a) The fluorescence quenching rate of Py-U-COFs-3 to different ions and the corresponding photographs under the UV lamp. b) The quenching rate of building units, Py-COFs, and Py-U-COFs-3 to the mercury(II). The concentration of mercury(II) and metal ions was 300 ppb.

(a) XPS survey spectra of Py-U-COFs-3 before and after binding of mercury(II). (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of Hg 4d on Py-U-COFs-3. High-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s of Py-U-COFs-3 before (c) and after (d) mercury(II) binding. High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s of Py-U-COFs-3 before (e) and after (f) mercury(II) binding.
(a) TEM image of Py-U-COFs-3. (b–f) EDS mapping graphs of C, N, O, Hg in Py-U-COFs-3-Hg, respectively.

head.ce:title: An in-situ strategy to construct uracil-conjugated covalent organic frameworks with tunable fluorescence/recognition characteristics for sensitive and selective Mercury(II) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang 330031, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Nuclear Resources and Environment, East China University of Technology (ECUT), Nanchang, 330013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Nuclear Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Nuclear Resources and Environment, East China University of Technology (ECUT), Nanchang 330013, P. R, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Previous researches of covalent organic frameworks (COFs) have shown their potential as fluorescent probes, but the regulation of their optical properties and recognition characteristics still remains a challenge, and most of reports required complicated post-decoration to improve the sensing performance. In this context, we propose a novel in-situ strategy to construct uracil-conjugated COFs and modulate their fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective mercury(II) detection. By using 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-formylphenyl)pyrene (TFPPy) and 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-aminophenyl)pyrene (TAPPy) as fundamental blocks and 5-aminouraci (5-AU) as the functional monomer, a series of COFs (Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5) with tunable fluorescence were solvothermally synthesized through an in-situ Schiff base reaction. The π-conjugated framework serves as a signal reporter, the evenly and densely distributed uracil acts as a mercury(II) receptor, and the regular pores (channels) make the rapid and sensitive detection of the mercury(II) possible. In this research, we manage to regulate the crystalline structure, the fluorescence properties, and the sensing performance of COFs by simply changing the molar ratio of precursors. We expect this research to open up a new strategy for effective and controllable construction of functionalized COFs for environmental analysis.

An in-situ strategy is proposed to construct uracil-conjugated COFs and modulate their fluorescence properties for sensitive and selective mercury(II) detection. The π-conjugated framework serves as a signal reporter, the evenly and densely distributed uracil acts as a mercury(II) receptor, and the regular pores (channels) make the rapid and sensitive detection of the mercury(II) possible.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is a heavy metal with bio-accumulative and high toxicity, responsible for environmental pollution and severe human diseases [1]. Moreover, the spreading mercury cycling brings more and more challenges to the environment worldwide [2,3]. In this context, detecting mercury in the environment is essential for protecting human health and ecological environments. Presently, the conventional assays to detect mercury(II) such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), must rely on large instruments that are not portable and limit the application. On the contrary, fluorescence measurement is a promising method for mercury(II) detection for its high sensitivity and good selectivity. In recent years, considerable efforts have been made to develop novel materials such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), mesoporous silica, DNAs, carbon dots, and hydrogel microparticles for sensitive detection and removal of mercury(II) [4–11]. However, the specificity of these probes toward mercury(II) and the generality of the strategies designed for other targets need to be improved.

COFs are porous crystalline materials constructed by rigid building blocks, with a large specific surface and stable skeleton [12]. The various modified monomers can predefine the skeleton, pore size, and pore shape of the COFs [13,14]. Researches over the past decade have developed lots of COFs with different structures and functions for wide applications, including energy storage [15,16], photocatalytic hydrogen generation [17], catalysis [18–20], membrane separation [21], sensing [22–24], and metal-ion removing [25–27]. Especially, with designable skeleton/pore size and diverse functional groups, COFs are potential materials for heavy metal ion detection. Compared to the traditional chemical reaction methods to detect mercury(II) in aqueous solutions, COFs are more superior and sensitive owing to their porous structure and unique electronic properties, and some previous researches have achieved sensitive detection of mercury(II) by COFs [28–31]. However, most of these COFs-based sensors need post-modification of functional groups or decoration with other nanomaterials to endow them with optical responses toward mercury(II) [25,32–37]. Thus far, there have not been any reports that can simultaneously realize the preparation, functionalization, and modulation of fluorescent COFs for efficient mercury(II) sensing.

Inspired by the unique base-binding property of mercury(II) with thymine (T) to form T-Hg-T complexes at room temperature [38], herein we propose an in-situ strategy to construct a series of uracil-conjugated COFs (Py-U-COFs) with tunable fluorescence characteristics and adjustable recognition performance for mercury(II) sensing. Although poly T-DNA probes or T-modified fluorescent nanoparticles have been previously investigated for mercury(II) detection [39–41], DNAs suffer from instability under extreme chemical environments while the post-modification strategies usually involve complex/time-consuming processes and produce limited recognition sites. It is expected that the present in-situ synthesis method can effectively compensate for these shortcomings and retain the crystalline structure of pristine materials while endowing them with sufficient binding sites and excellent recognition performance.

All reagents were obtained from commercial sources and used as received. 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-formylphenyl)pyrene (TFPPy) and 1,3,6,8-tetrakis(4-aminophenyl)pyrene (TAPPy) were purchased from Jilin Chinese Academy of Science-Yanshen Technology Co., Ltd. 5-Aminouracil (5-AU) was purchased from J&K SCIENTIFIC. The 1H NMR spectra of TFPPy, TAPPy, and 5-AU were shown in Fig. S1. CH2Cl2, tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), acetic acid (HAc) and other common reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was prepared from the Millipore system (18.25 MΩ cm). All the purchased reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Impact 410 FT-IR spectrometer. The morphology of the material was imaged by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM, FEI) and a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, ASAP-2460). Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were collected on a Bruker AXS D8 Advance A25 powder X-ray diffractometer (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) in the range from 2° to 60°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on a Thermo VG Multilab 2000X with Al Kα irradiation. 13C NMR spectra were checked by Varian instruments (400 MHz). The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 M system. The samples were outgassed at 120 °C for 12 h before the measurements. Surface areas were calculated from the adsorption data using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) methods. The pore-size-distribution curves were obtained via the nonlocal density functional theory (NLDFT) method. The thermal properties of the materials were evaluated using a STA PT1600 Linseis thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) instrument over the temperature range of 30–800 °C under nitrogen atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on an UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded on a FL spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The FL lifetime was measured using an FL3-P-TCSPC time-resolved FL spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Longjumeau, France). The fluorescencce quantum yields were measured by a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments). The fluorescence images of living cells were captured on a microscope (Leica DM6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of TFPPy (30.9 mg, 0.05 mmol), TAPPy (28.3 mg, 0.05 mmol), and 1,2-dichlorobenzene/1-butanol (1:1, 1.0 mL) in a 15 mL glass tube was sonicated for 15 min, then 0.2 mL of acetic acid solution (6 M) was added. The tube was flash frozen in a liquid nitrogen bath, degassed by three freeze-pump-thaw cycles and flame sealed under vacuum. Upon warming to room temperature, the sealed tube was placed in an oven at 120 °C and left undisturbed for 3 days. The formed precipitate was collected by filtration, washed successively with DMF, THF and CH2Cl2, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h to produce a yellowish brown solid of Py-COFs. Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5 were prepared by a similar method as that for Py-COFs, except that a part of TAPPy was replaced by 5-AU. Among them, the molar ratio of amino groups of 5-AU in Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5 is 10% (2.5 mg, 0.02 mmol), 30% (7.6 mg, 0.06 mmol), 50% (12.7 mg, 0.1 mmol), 70% (17.8 mg, 0.14 mmol), and 90% (22.9 mg, 0.18 mmol), respectively. The filtered solid gradually turns from yellowish brown to yellowish green.

Py-U–COF–3 was readily dispersed in ultrapure water and the obtained yellowish suspension was almost transparent. For the detection of mercury(II), under the optimal pH value of 6.0, 50 μL of the stock Py-U–COF–3 solution (0.08 mg/mL) was mixed with mercury(II) aqueous solutions with different final concentrations, and the total volume was 200 μL. Each test was repeated at least for three times to get concordant values. All the measurements, unless otherwise noted, were excited at λex = 360 nm and the corresponding emission wavelength was tested from λem = 390–680 nm. For the selectivity test, other metal ions, including Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Sr2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Cr6+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, and Cu2+, were used to investigate the selectivity of Py-U–COF–3. The metal ion solutions were prepared by dissolving the corresponding nitrate salts in ultrapure water. Typically, the metal ion solution with final concentration of 300 ppb was injected into 50 μL of the stock Py-U–COF–3 solution (0.08 mg/mL) in pH 6.0 buffer solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured. A mixture of 50 μL of the stock Py-U–COF–3 solution (0.08 mg/mL) in the absence of metal ions was used as the blank control.

The applicability of the proposed method was researched by using environmental water (the water from Ganjiang River in Jiangxi Province of China) as the real samples. The recovery was investigated by standard addition method. The water samples were firstly filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble impurities and then spiked with Hg2+ (50 ppb, 100 ppb, and 200 ppb). Subsequently, the sample solution was detected by the proposed method. The experiment of each sample was independently tested for three times.

The pyrene-based COFs (Py-COFs) were synthesized via Schiff base reaction between TFPPy and TAPPy by using 1:1 n-butylalcohol/1,2-dichlorobenzene as the solvent (Fig. 1 a and b and Fig. S2). The uracil-functionalized Py-COFs (Py-U-COFs) were in-situ prepared under the same conditions, and the proportions of 5-AU were varied at 10%, 30%, 50%, 70%, and 90% to produce Py-U-COFs-1, Py-U-COFs-2, Py-U-COFs-3, Py-U-COFs-4, and Py-U-COFs-5, respectively (Fig. 1c). Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs were obtained as powders, and their colors tend to turn from yellowish brown to yellowish green along with the increasing proportion of 5-AU.

To confirm the successful synthesis of Py-COFs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and 13C cross-polarization magic-angle spinning (13C CP/MAS) NMR characterizations were performed. Compared with monomers, the appearance of the peak for CN at 1600 cm−1 with the concomitant disappearance of the aldehydic CO (1694 cm−1) stretching vibrations of TFPPy and the N–H (3344 cm−1) stretching vibrations and N–H (1272 cm−1) bending vibration of TAPPy indicates the successful synthesis via the Schiff base reaction (Fig. 2 a) [42,43]. Besides, the enhancement of the CO (1653 cm−1) stretching vibration of Py-U-COFs relative to Py-COFs and the appearance of N–H (1211 cm−1) bending vibration of Py-U-COFs indicate that 5-AU has been fixed onto the Py-U-COFs. The 13C CP/MAS NMR spectrum of Py-U-COFs reveal a characteristic resonance signal at 162 ppm that can be ascribed to the formation of CN, which confirms the successful synthesis of COFs [22]. The peak obtained at 152 ppm is ascribed to the carbonyl of uracil (Fig. 2b), indicating that uracil has been successfully fixed onto the COFs [44]. It is speculated that the presence of uracil leads to some defects on the Py-U-COFs and more aldehyde groups left without reaction [45]. Besides, the structure of the Py-U-COFs can be confirmed by the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis). The UV–vis absorption spectra show that the 5-AU exhibits a distinctive absorption peak at 292 nm, and this characteristic absorption band increases along with the series of Py-U-COFs-1, Py-U-COFs-2, Py-U-COFs-3, Py-U-COFs-4, and Py-U COFs-5, further proving that the uracil has been functionalized onto the skeleton of the COFs (Fig. S3).

The crystalline structure of Py-COFs was determined by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis in conjunction with computational structural simulations and Pawley refinement (Materials Studio, version 2019) (Fig. 2c). The experimental spectrum of Py-COFs shows a strong diffraction peak at 5.09° (2θ) attributed to the reflection from the (110) plane, indicating the highly crystalline skeleton of Py-COFs. The other peaks at 7.07° and 10.19° (2θ) are attributed to the reflections of (200) and (220), respectively. By using the density-functional tight-binding (DFTB+) method, the AA stacking mode of Py-COFs matches well with the PXRD pattern (Fig. 2c, green curve) and affords optimized parameters of a = 25.7460 Å, b = 23.4617 Å, c = 5.4403 Å, and α = β = γ = 90° for the unit cell (Fig. S4 and Table S1). The Pawley refinement yields a PXRD pattern (Fig. 2c, pink curve) with low RWP and RP values of 6.31% and 4.34% respectively, which matches well with the simulated pattern. It is noticeable that the PXRD patterns Py-U-COFs exhibit a decreasing intensity at 5.09° (2θ) along with Py-COFs, Py-U-COFs-1, Py-U-COFs-2, Py-U-COFs-3, Py-U-COFs-4, and Py-U-COFs-5 (Fig. S5), indicating that the introduction of uracil influences the crystalline structure of the COFs. When the proportion of 5-AU is below 50%, the Py-U-COFs retain a relatively good crystalline structure while the Py-U-COFs-5 shows negligible intensity on the PXRD pattern, suggesting the poor crystalline structure due to a high proportion of 5-AU.

The specific surface areas were evaluated by the Brunauer-Emmet-Teller (BET) method. The Py-U-COFs-3 exhibited a similar N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm to that of Py-COFs (Fig. 2d), while the specific surface area of Py-U-COFs-3 decreased to be 398.2 m2/g compared to that of Py-COFs with a high specific surface area of 1008.9 m2/g, which can be attributed to the presence of uracil groups projecting into the material [46]. The pore size distributions of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-3 in Fig. S6 suggest their layered porous structure. The average pore diameters of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-3 are 2.69 nm and 4.08 nm respectively, and the increase in pore diameter of Py-U-COFs-3 can be attributed to the skeletal terminals of uracil wrapped by the COFs skeleton, which merges the adjacent pores and forms a larger cavity [47]. The above results demonstrate that the incorporation of uracil inside Py-COFs leads to a decrease in specific surface area and an increase in pore size.

The morphology of the materials can be characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The SEM images of Py-COFs, Py-U-COFs-1, Py-U-COFs-2, and Py-U-COFs-3 reveal a puffy and brush-like morphology (Fig. 3 a–d), while the SEM images of Py-U-COFs-4 and Py-U-COFs-5 displayed a compact and irregular morphology (Fig. 3e and f). The SEM images match well with the PXRD patterns and provide further evidence that the introduction of low contents of 5-AU can hardly affect the skeleton structure of the COFs while the large proportions of 5-AU will destroy the crystallinity and change their morphologies. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) revealed the excellent thermal stability of Py-COFs. As the pattern showed (Fig. S7), Py-COFs can maintain 90% of the weight under 600 °C while Py-U-COFs-3 can retain 68% of the weight under the same temperature. The declining thermal stability upon the uracil modification coincides with the PXRD and morphology evolutions. The chemical stability of Py-U-COFs-3 was then investigated by immersing the material in different solvents including ethanol, THF, DMF, HCl (1 M), and NaOH (1 M) for 12 h. The PXRD patterns demonstrated that Py-U-COFs-3 retained good crystallinity under these severe conditions, indicating the good chemical stability of Py-U-COFs-3 (Fig. S8).

The fluorescence properties of Py-COFs and Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5 were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. 4 a–f). The aqueous solution of Py-COFs, with weak fluorescence intensity, emerges the maximum emission wavelength at 549 nm (λex = 360 nm). With the gradual increase of the 5-AU proportion in in-situ synthesis, the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm gradually increases, and the shoulder peak at 540 nm gets more and more distinctive (λex = 360 nm). As shown in Fig. 4g, as the ratio of 5-AU in the precursor increases, the color of the dispersion of the products gradually changes from orange to yellow under natural light. The fluorescent color of the Py-U-COFs aqueous solution evolves from blue to white and finally to yellow under an UV lamp with λex = 365 nm (Fig. 4h). The spectral and the photo results suggest that the fluorescence yield and the emission color of the Py-U-COFs can be adjusted simply by changing the ratio of 5-AU in the precursors, which can be further confirmed by the evolution of fluorescence quantum yields (0.07%, 0.53%, 1.26%, 2.45%, and 4.69% for Py-U-COFs-1 to Py-U-COFs-5, respectively). Notably, the aqueous solution of the monomers of TAPPy or the 5-AU demonstrates negligible fluorescence emission under the same conditions (Fig. S9), indicating that the construction of the π-conjugation frameworks, as well as the functionalization of uracil, endows the materials with good water dispersibility and bright fluorescence emission.

We conceive that the functional COFs could be used to detect mercury(II). First, the organic building units have a π-conjugation structure, and the generated COFs show strong fluorescence after polymerization. The excellent optical performance makes COFs potential as photo-sensing elements. In this regard, the rigid COF framework serves not only for structural preservation but also for the signaling function. Second, the porous crystalline structure offers an excellent scaffold for hosting mercury(II) for detection. Therefore, easy access to the binding sites within the regular channels is guaranteed for a sensitive response. Third, taking advantages of the porous structure and the excellent fluorescence properties of COFs and based on the specific interaction between uracil and mercury(II) [38,48,49], we envision that Py-U-COFs can specifically recognize mercury(II) and cause the fluorescence change.

Firstly, the effect of pH on the interaction between mercury(II) and Py-U-COFs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S10, with a fixed mercury(II) concentration, Py-U-COFs-3 showed the highest quenching rate at around pH 6.0. It is speculated that the decrease of fluorescence quenching rate under relatively strong acid conditions can be attributed to the protonation of 5-AU, which may prevent the coordination between 5-AU and mercury(II). Besides, the decrease of the fluoresccence quenching rate under alkaline conditions should be ascribed to the hydrolysis of mercury(II) [4,50]. As a consequence of lacking uracil as mercury(II) receptors, the fluorescence quenching effect of Py-COFs is negligible (Fig. 5 a). On the contrary, under the optimal pH condition, Py-U-COFs embedded with evenly and densely distributed uracil as the active binding sites show efficient fluorescence quenching with the increasing of mercury(II) concentrations (Fig. 5b–f). The fluorescence signal of Py-U-COFs-1, Py-U-COFs-2, and Py-U-COFs-3 linearly responses to mercury(II) in the range of 40–140 ppb, 4–100 ppb, and 4–280 ppb, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of Py-U-COFs-3 can be quenched by 70% with the mercury(II) concentration of 280 ppb and the quantum yield of Py-U-COFs-3 decreased to be 0.06%, and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 1.38 ppb (3σ), which is close to the drinking water guideline values set by WHO and Pak-EPA (1 ppb) [51,52]. Compared with the previous studies shown in Table S2, Py-U-COFs-3 displays a better or comparable sensing performance for mercury(II) detection. It is worth noting that when the 5-AU ratio increases to be a higher level, although the fluorescence intensity of Py-U-COFs is stronger, its response to mercury(II) drastically weakens. For instance, for Py-U-COFs-4, the linear detection range for mercury(II) is 80–220 ppb with the LOD of 71.23 ppb. Moreover, when the 5-AU ratio increases to 90%, even if the concentration of mercury(II) increases to be 300 ppb, the fluorescence of Py-U-COFs-5 can be just quenched by 36% (Fig. 5f). The reason for that may be the increasing 5-AU ratio increases the number of active sites, but the disintegration of the ordered crystal structure of COFs leads to the reduction or disappearance of the ordered crystallinity/pore structure (this point can be confirmed by the evolution of PXRD patterns and morphology of SEM images), which is unfavorable for transmission of mercury(II) to the uracil binding sites, resulting in a decrease in the quenching efficiency of the pyrene fluorophores.

The selectivity of Py-U-COFs-3 toward the mercury(II) detection was then investigated by examining the fluorescence change in the presence of other metal ions. As shown in Fig. 6 a, among all the tested metal ions, only mercury(II) caused significant fluorescence quenching of Py-U-COFs-3, which can be observed by naked eyes with the solution color change from white to light yellow under a portable UV lamp (λ = 365 nm). It is speculated that the addition of mercury(II) results in the fluorescence quenching of Py-U-COFs-3 at about 450 nm, and thus the solution exhibits yellow fluorescence at 540 nm. Further studies revealed that the mixture of mercury(II) and other ions or humic acid at a 1:5 ratio exhibited comparable fluorescence quenching rate to that of mercury(II) alone (Fig. S11), further confirming the good selectivity of the method for mercury(II) detection. In addition, we compared the fluorescence responses of the two monomers (TFPPy and TAPPy), 5-AU and Py-COFs to mercury(II) (Fig. 6b). The aqueous solution of TAPPy, 5-AU, and Py-COFs displays negligible fluorescence, and the addition of mercury(II) can hardly cause the fluorescence change. Although the TFPPy aqueous solution demonstrates fluorescence emission, the presence of mercury(II) cannot quench the fluorescence. Only the aqueous solution of Py-U-COFs-3 with a high fluorescence intensity can recognize mercury(II) by the fluorescence quenching. It is supposed that uracil on the surface of Py-U-COFs-3 can specifically bind to mercury(II), resulting in the quenching of pyrene fluorophores in COFs. The different responses of these materials toward mercury(II) highlight the uniqueness of using uracil-conjugated fluorescent COFs for mercury(II) sensing with excellent specificity.

To demonstrate the practical application of the prepared Py-U-COFs-3, environmental water from the Ganjiang River in Jiangxi Province was investigated as the real sample. The obtained water was first filtered through 0.22 μm membranes to remove impurities. Then the standard mercury(II) solutions with concentrations of 50 ppb, 100 ppb, and 200 ppb were added to the mixture of Py-U-COFs-3 and real water sample. The mercury(II) concentrations in the prepared samples were measured to be 51.27 ppb, 106.73 ppb, and 204.74 ppb (Table S3), respectively, and the satisfactory recovery indicates that the soluble impurities in real water have negligible influence on the mercury(II) responses. The above results suggest the potential of Py-U-COFs-3 as a fluorescent probe in real water sample detection. The potential of the present method in monitoring mercury(II) in living cells was further investigated. The two types of cultured cells were treated with Py-U-COFs-3 for 30 min followed by exposure to mercury(II) for 30 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence microscopic images revealed that the fluorescence intensity of Py-U-COFs can be almost completely quenched by 300 ppb or more mercury(II), verifying the feasibility of the method in living samples (Fig. S12).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was then applied to explore the mercury(II) binding mechanism. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the survey spectrum of Py-U-COFs-3 certifies the presence of C, N, and O, and the survey spectrum after mercury(II) binding presents a weak mercury-specific peak at 360–380 eV. The XPS signature of the Hg 4d3/2 and Hg 4d5/2 doublet at 379.50 eV and 360.10 eV suggests that mercury(II) has been successfully bonded onto Py-U-COFs-3 (Fig. 7b). The deconvolution of N 1s and O 1s spectra before and after mercury(II) binding was investigated to verify the hypothesis that mercury(II) mainly coordinates with the uracil on Py-COFs. As shown in Fig. 7c–d, the N 1s region of the Py-U-COFs-3 (Fig. 7c) was deconvoluted into three chemically distinct nitrogen species centered at 398.80 eV, 399.49 eV, and 401.15 eV, which can be attributed to CN, C–N3, and C–N1, respectively [53,54]. The peak separation of the two C–N bonds is due to the similar chemical states of the two N atoms in uracil [55]. For N 1s, the more positive N in the compounds, the higher the electron binding energy is [56]. It is worth noticing that upon the binding of mercury(II), the positive shift of C–N3 (Fig. 7d) proves the interaction between N and mercury(II): electrons were donated to mercury(II), which leads to the N atoms electron-deficient [46]. Such a transform in binding energy indicates that mercury(II) has been coordinated with N3 atoms of uracil on Py-U-COFs-3. Moreover, the increased peak proportion of C–N1 and CN and the decreased peak proportion of C–N3 provide further evidence for the coordination between mercury(II) and N3 [57,58]. The deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum of Py-U-COFs-3 displays two peaks centered at 531.92 eV and 533.36 eV (Fig. 7e), which attributes to CO bonds and C–O bonds, respectively [5]. After interaction with mercury(II), the C–O peak negatively shifts by 0.70 eV, proving that the oxygen atoms play a crucial role in coordinating with mercury(II). The coordination of mercury(II) to the O of uracil on Py-U-COFs-3 can be further confirmed through the decrease of the CO peak proportion and the increase of the C–O peak proportion [57,58]. Based on the above XPS analysis, it can be concluded that both N and O atoms of uracil on Py-U-COFs-3 are involved in coordinating mercury(II) [59].

To gain a deeper understanding, Py-U-COFs-3 was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping. As shown in Fig. 8 a, Py-U-COFs-3 displays brush-like morphology that is consistent with the SEM results. The EDS mapping of C, N, and O elements revealed a uniform distribution on the Py-U-COFs-3 (Fig. 8c, d, e), and the relatively sparse distribution of mercury(II) element indicates that the mercury(II) has been successfully coordinated to the Py-U-COFs-3 (Fig. 8f). It is believed that the mercury(II) coordinates to the O and N on the 5-AU of Py-U-COFs-3 and forms a non-fluorescent complex [60]. As shown in Fig. S13 and Table S4 in the Supporting Information, the fluorescence lifetime of Py-U-COFs-3 is 8.26 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime shows negligible change after treating with the mercury(II) (8.34 ns), indicating the static quenching mechanism as a result of the coordination of Py-U-COFs-3 with mercury(II) to form non-fluorescent species [61].

In conclusion, an in-situ method has been proposed to simultaneously prepare, regulate, and functionalize COFs by using TFPPy, TAPPy, and different ratios of 5-AU as monomers. The crystallinity structure, the optical property as well as the fluorescence response to mercury(II) of the COFs can be modulated by simply varying the proportion of precursors. Specially, the Py-U-COFs-3 exhibits mercury(II) recognition performance with satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity, and the LOD is as low as 1.38 ppb. Moreover, other common coexisting ions do not affect the mercury(II) sensing, facilitating its potential applications in real water sample detection. Its outstanding response performance comes from the unique crystallinity structure of COFs, the specific binding of uracil to mercury(II), and the resulting static quenching principle. The present contribution provides a novel in-situ strategy for COFs functionalization, which endows COFs controllable fluorescence and recognition characteristics, providing important reference significance for the modulation and application of COFs in the future.

Xi-Rui Deng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Draft preparation. A-Wei Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Draft preparation. Sheng-Qian Hu: Conceptualization. Wen-Li Yang: Conceptualization. Chen Sun: Revision, Discussion. Sai-Jin Xiao: Formal analysis, Discussion, Funding acquisition. Gui-Ping Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Qiong-Qing Zheng: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ru-Ping Liang: Formal analysis, Discussion, Funding acquisition. Li Zhang: Discussion, Supervision, Formal analysis, Validation, Revision, Funding acquisition. Jian-Ding Qiu: Discussion, Supervision, Formal analysis, Validation, Revision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21964011, 22166024, 22064001 and 22036003) and Jiangxi Provincial Natural Science Foundation (20212ACB203009 and 20212ACB203011).
